criteria: The agent has read Section 2.1 completely to understand the collaborativeness of LLMs — i.e., LLMs generate better responses when provided outputs from other models, even if those auxiliary outputs are of lower quality.

The agent has read Section 2.2 completely to understand the MoA architecture including Equation 1: y_i = ⊕_{j=1}^{n}[A_{i,j}(x_i)] + x_1, x_{i+1} = y_i, and the two roles of Proposers and Aggregators.
The agent has read Table 1 to understand the exact Aggregate-and-Synthesize prompt template that instructs the aggregator to synthesize a single high-quality response from multiple model outputs.

The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the evaluation setup: benchmarks (AlpacaEval 2.0, Arena-Hard, MT-Bench, FLASK), the 6 open-source proposer models, and the three MoA variants (MoA, MoA w/ GPT-4o, MoA-Lite).

The agent has read Section 3.3 to understand the analysis experiments: MoA vs LLM ranker, BLEU correlation, regression analysis (aggregator coefficient 0.588 vs proposer 0.281), diversity experiments, and role specialization.

The agent has read Table 3 to understand the diversity experiment design: varying n (number of proposers) from 1 to 6 and comparing single-proposer (same LLM, temperature 0.7) vs multiple-proposer settings with 2 MoA layers and Qwen1.5-110B-Chat as aggregator.
The agent has read Table 5 to understand the comparison with existing multi-agent methods (MAD and Reconcile) in terms of AlpacaEval2 LC score and average cost at 1 and 2 aggregation rounds.
The agent has read Appendix E to understand that MoA is evaluated on reasoning tasks: MATH, Big-Bench Hard (BBH), MMLU, and CSQA, using the MoA-Lite configuration.

The agent has read Appendix F to understand the architecture search: fixing 2 layers and Qwen1.5-110B-Chat as aggregator, using BFGS optimization on 50 AlpacaEval + 50 Arena-Hard validation problems to select which proposer models to use.

The plan includes implementation of the l-layer MoA architecture where each layer i has n LLM agents A_{i,1},...,A_{i,n} that independently generate responses, which are concatenated and fed to the next layer.

The plan specifies that the Aggregate-and-Synthesize prompt from Table 1 must be prepended as a system prompt to the aggregator at each layer, with numbered model responses appended, and the original user query following immediately after.

The plan includes the default MoA configuration: 6 proposers (Qwen1.5-110B-Chat, Qwen1.5-72B-Chat, WizardLM-8x22B, LLaMA-3-70B-Instruct, Mixtral-8x22B-v0.1, dbrx-instruct) with 3 layers and Qwen1.5-110B-Chat as the final-layer aggregator.
The plan includes the MoA-Lite configuration: same 6 proposers but only 2 MoA layers and Qwen1.5-72B-Chat as the final aggregator.
The plan includes the MoA w/ GPT-4o configuration: same proposers as MoA but with GPT-4o (gpt-4o-2024-05-13) as the final-layer aggregator.
The plan includes evaluation on AlpacaEval 2.0 using length-controlled (LC) win rate metric, comparing against GPT-4 (gpt-4-1106-preview) responses with a GPT-4-based evaluator over 805 instructions.

The plan includes the diversity experiment: varying n (1, 2, 3, 6 proposers) in both multiple-proposer and single-proposer settings using Qwen1.5-110B-Chat as aggregator with 2 MoA layers on AlpacaEval 2.0.

The plan includes the proposer/aggregator role analysis experiment: evaluating each of the 6 models independently as aggregator (with all 6 as proposers) and as proposer (with Qwen1.5-110B-Chat as aggregator, using 2 layers).

The plan includes the LLM ranker baseline: using the aggregator model (Qwen1.5-110B-Chat with the prompt from Appendix C) to select the best response from the proposers rather than synthesizing a new one.

The plan includes comparison experiments with MAD and Reconcile multi-agent methods, adapting their prompts for open-ended chat and measuring AlpacaEval2 LC score and cost at 1 and 2 aggregation rounds.

The plan includes the BLEU correlation analysis: for each sample, computing Spearman rank correlation between n BLEU similarity scores (3-gram, 4-gram, 5-gram) of proposed outputs vs. aggregator output and the GPT-4 preference scores of those proposed outputs.

The core MoA pipeline is implemented as a multi-layer system where: (1) first-layer agents independently generate responses to the user query; (2) each subsequent layer receives the concatenated previous-layer outputs prepended with the Aggregate-and-Synthesize prompt; (3) the final-layer single aggregator's output is used as the result.

The Aggregate-and-Synthesize prompt (Table 1) is implemented exactly: it instructs the model to synthesize a single high-quality response from the numbered list of provided model outputs, critically evaluating potential biases, without simply replicating answers.
The proposer role is implemented such that each LLM A_{i,j} independently receives the input prompt x_i and generates its response without seeing the outputs of other models in the same layer.

The aggregator role is implemented such that the aggregator receives all n proposer outputs concatenated with the Aggregate-and-Synthesize system prompt, plus the original user query x_1, and synthesizes a single refined response.

LLM inference is implemented via the Together Inference Endpoint API for all open-source models (Qwen1.5-110B-Chat, Qwen1.5-72B-Chat, WizardLM-8x22B, LLaMA-3-70B-Instruct, Mixtral-8x22B-v0.1, dbrx-instruct) without any fine-tuning.

The default MoA configuration is implemented with exactly 3 layers, using the same set of 6 models (Qwen1.5-110B-Chat, Qwen1.5-72B-Chat, WizardLM-8x22B, LLaMA-3-70B-Instruct, Mixtral-8x22B-v0.1, dbrx-instruct) in each layer, with Qwen1.5-110B-Chat as the single aggregator in the final layer.
MoA-Lite is implemented with 2 MoA layers using the same 6 proposer models and Qwen1.5-72B-Chat (not Qwen1.5-110B-Chat) as the final aggregator, producing a more cost-effective variant.
MoA w/ GPT-4o is implemented with the same proposer configuration as MoA but uses gpt-4o-2024-05-13 as the final-layer aggregator (GPT-4o does not participate as proposer toward the next layer).

The single-proposer setting is implemented where n responses in each MoA layer are generated by the same LLM using temperature sampling (temperature=0.7), as the control condition for comparing against the multiple-proposer diverse setting.

The AlpacaEval 2.0 evaluation pipeline is implemented: generating responses for 805 instructions, comparing against GPT-4 (gpt-4-1106-preview) baseline responses using a GPT-4-based judge, and computing the length-controlled (LC) win rate.
The Arena-Hard evaluation pipeline is implemented for 500 challenging user queries covering diverse topics (coding, math, logic), producing win-rate scores against baseline.
The MT-Bench evaluation pipeline is implemented: using GPT-4 as a judge to score model responses on both first-turn and second-turn questions, producing scores on a 0-10 scale.

The LLM ranker baseline (Appendix C) is implemented: it uses Qwen1.5-110B-Chat with a ranking prompt that presents all proposer outputs and asks the model to output the identifier of the best response, without generating a new synthesized response.

The diversity and proposer-count experiment is implemented: for n ∈ {1, 2, 3, 6}, running both single-proposer (same model, temperature 0.7) and multiple-proposer (different models) configurations with Qwen1.5-110B-Chat as aggregator and 2 MoA layers, evaluated on AlpacaEval 2.0.

The proposer vs. aggregator role analysis is implemented: for each of the 6 models, (a) using it as the sole aggregator with all 6 models as proposers, and (b) using it as a proposer 6 times (single-proposer) with Qwen1.5-110B-Chat as aggregator, both with 2 MoA layers.

The BLEU similarity vs. win-rate Spearman correlation analysis is implemented: for each AlpacaEval 2.0 sample, computing BLEU (3-gram, 4-gram, 5-gram) between each proposer output and the aggregator output, then computing Spearman correlation with GPT-4 preference scores.

Linear regression analysis is implemented: fitting a regression line between each model's standalone LC win rate (x-axis) and the final MoA LC win rate when used in each role (y-axis), separately for aggregator and proposer roles, and extracting the regression coefficients.

Comparison baselines MAD (Multi-Agent Debate) and Reconcile are implemented for open-ended chat: adapting their original prompts for chatting by removing JSON outputs and confidence levels (Reconcile), evaluated at 1 and 2 rounds of aggregation on AlpacaEval 2.0.

Cost analysis is implemented: computing average inference cost per AlpacaEval 2.0 instance for each MoA variant using pricing information from API providers, accounting for input and output token counts across all layers.

Reasoning task evaluation is implemented for BBH (23 multi-step tasks), MMLU (57-task knowledge/reasoning), CSQA (commonsense QA), and MATH dataset, using the MoA-Lite configuration (2 layers, Qwen1.5-110B-Chat aggregator).

Small model experiments are implemented: using gemma-2-9b, llama-3.1-8b, mistral-7b-instruct-v0.3, and qwen-1.5-7b as proposers, with each model also evaluated separately as aggregator across 1, 2, and 3 MoA layers on AlpacaEval 2.0, Arena-Hard, and MT-Bench.

The architecture search is implemented: fixing 2 layers and Qwen1.5-110B-Chat as aggregator, using BFGS optimization over proposer selection variables, evaluated on 100 validation samples (50 AlpacaEval + 50 Arena-Hard) with the average score as the optimization metric.

The MoA pipeline with the default configuration (3 layers, 6 models, Qwen1.5-110B-Chat aggregator) and MoA-Lite (2 layers, Qwen1.5-72B-Chat aggregator) are executed successfully on all 805 AlpacaEval 2.0 instructions without errors.

The MoA pipeline is executed on Arena-Hard (500 queries) and MT-Bench for all three MoA variants (MoA, MoA-Lite, MoA w/ GPT-4o) without errors, with experiments repeated 3 times to obtain mean and standard deviation.
The diversity experiments (Table 3) with varying n and single- vs. multiple-proposer settings are executed successfully on AlpacaEval 2.0.
The proposer vs. aggregator role analysis experiments (Table 4) are executed successfully for all 6 models on AlpacaEval 2.0 with 2 MoA layers.
The LLM ranker, MAD, and Reconcile comparison experiments (Table 5) are executed successfully on AlpacaEval 2.0 at 1 and 2 aggregation rounds.
The reasoning task experiments (Table 9) are executed successfully on BBH, MMLU, CSQA, and MATH using the MoA-Lite setup.

The main AlpacaEval 2.0 LC win rate results match those in Table 2: MoA achieves approximately 65.1% (±0.6%), MoA w/ GPT-4o achieves 65.7% (±0.7%), MoA-Lite achieves 59.3% (±0.2%), all outperforming GPT-4o (57.5%) and GPT-4 Turbo (55.0%).
The Arena-Hard results match Table 2: MoA w/ GPT-4o achieves 90.3% (±0.5%), MoA achieves 77.4% (±0.5%), MoA-Lite achieves 71.3% (±0.7%), surpassing GPT-4o's 79.2% (for MoA w/ GPT-4o).
The MT-Bench results match Table 2: MoA w/ GPT-4o achieves 9.40 (±0.06) average, MoA achieves 9.25 (±0.10), MoA-Lite achieves 9.18 (±0.09), with first-turn and second-turn scores reported separately.

The diversity experiment results match Table 3: with Qwen1.5-110B-Chat aggregator and 2 layers, multiple-proposer at n=6 achieves 61.3% vs. single-proposer 56.7%; at n=1 both converge to 47.8%; scores increase monotonically with n.

The role analysis results match Table 4: WizardLM-8x22B achieves the highest proposer score (63.8%) but a relatively low aggregator score (52.9%); Qwen1.5-110B-Chat achieves the highest aggregator score (61.3%).

The linear regression analysis confirms that the regression coefficient for aggregator models (0.588) is higher than for proposer models (0.281), indicating the final MoA performance is more sensitive to aggregator quality than proposer quality.

The comparison with existing methods (Table 5) matches: with 1 aggregation, MoA scores 61.3 vs. MAD 53.5 and Reconcile 47.6; with 2 aggregations, MoA scores 65.7 vs. MAD 50.6 and Reconcile 47.7 on AlpacaEval2 LC, while maintaining similar cost.
The BLEU similarity Spearman correlation results confirm a positive correlation between proposer output BLEU similarity to the aggregator and GPT-4 preference scores, consistent with Figure 5(b).
The MoA significantly outperforms the LLM ranker baseline across all MoA layers in Figure 5(a), validating that the aggregator performs synthesis rather than mere selection.

The reasoning task results match Table 9: MoA-Lite achieves 0.7667 on BBH, 0.8268 on MMLU, 0.8444 on CSQA, 0.570 on MATH, and 0.752 average — outperforming all individual models including Wizard 8x22b (0.719).
The small model results match Table 6: using gemma-2-9b as aggregator achieves up to 56.83% LC win rate at 3 layers, demonstrating consistent improvement with more layers (48.54% → 56.54% → 56.83%).

The cost-efficiency analysis shows MoA-Lite at approximately $0.005/query outperforms GPT-4o at $0.008/query on AlpacaEval 2.0 LC win rate, and MoA at ~$0.032 achieves a higher score than GPT-4 Turbo at ~$0.012, consistent with Figure 6(a).
